text: The liver retractor is positioned on the left lobe of the liver, visible at the top of the screen. The stomach is pulled out of the chest, with the hernia sac being delivered from the chest. The stomach is prominently visible in the main view and is maneuvered to the right of the screen. Cautery is used to divide the tissue attaching the stomach to the chest, facilitating its delivery.

The surgeon closes the defect in the diaphragm, through which the stomach previously traveled, by placing permanent sutures on each side of the hole. Four sutures are used to complete the hiatal hernia repair.

After closing the diaphragmatic defect, the surgeon divides the short gastric vessels on the outer lining of the stomach to mobilize it. The stomach is then passed behind the esophagus and grasped from the opposite side to prepare for the Nissen fundoplication.

In the background, mesh is visible from the hiatal hernia repair. The surgeon performs a shoe shine maneuver to position the stomach behind the esophagus for the fundoplication. Sutures are placed between the marked areas of the stomach, incorporating small bites of the esophageal muscular lining to secure the wrap and prevent slipping. An additional suture is added for reinforcement.
The final view shows the completed Nissen fundoplication in its natural position, with the stomach wrapped around the esophagus to prevent acid reflux.
